content:
{
  "title": "Counterfactual Prompting: Dynamically Reducing Social Biases in LLM Outputs via On-the-Fly Contrastive Prompts",
  "problem": "Large language models often produce outputs that reflect or amplify social biases and stereotypes, even in the presence of instruction tuning or RLHF. Existing prompting techniques have limited effectiveness in systematically mitigating these biases across diverse contexts.",
  "motivation": "Reducing social bias in LLM outputs is critical for deploying these models ethically and safely in real-world applications. As models are integrated into sensitive domains, prompt-based mitigation strategies offer rapid and cost-effective solutions without retraining. New prompting methods that generalize across contexts and demographics are urgently needed.",
  "method": "We propose 'Counterfactual Prompting': for each input, the user provides a minimally perturbed, demographically contrasted version of the prompt (e.g., changing gender/race variables). The LLM is prompted with both the original and counterfactual prompts in a joint context, and instructed to align its outputs to ensure equivalent, stereotype-free responses. A contrastive loss is computed on token-level generations, and optional self-critique can be included via a separate analysis prompt. This method is modular, requiring no model fine-tuning and leverages only prompt engineering.",
  "experiment_plan": "We evaluate on StereoSet and CrowS-Pairs, measuring bias scores and answer equivalence across demographic axes. Baselines include standard prompting, instruction tuning, and chain-of-thought self-critique. Metrics include stereotype score reduction, semantic fidelity, and human preference ratings for bias/fairness."
}
